---
title: "Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"
subtitle: "Overview"
---

# Objective

This chapter introduces the discipline behind a usable backtest:

1. independently verify even published strategies
2. connect backtesting assumptions to automated execution assumptions
3. separate exploratory model revision from true out-of-sample testing
4. audit the data, venue, and implementation pitfalls that inflate results

# Sections

## 1.1 The Importance of Backtesting

[Open web notes](sections/01-the-importance-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)

The first section explains why traders should independently backtest strategies,
including published strategies, before trusting reported performance.

Outputs:
[video](sections/01-the-importance-of-backtesting/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[web](sections/01-the-importance-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd),
[PDF](sections/01-the-importance-of-backtesting/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting

[Open web notes](sections/02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)

The second section reviews look-ahead bias, data-snooping bias, corporate
actions, survivorship bias, consolidated stock prices, and venue-specific
currency quotes.

Outputs:
[video](sections/02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[web](sections/02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd),
[PDF](sections/02-common-pitfalls-of-backtesting/pdf-notes.qmd)

# Output Links

Each section owns its own video slides, web notes, and PDF notes. Use the
section folders as the source of truth; shared chapter pages are only indexes.
